title: Roast Crown of Lamb
subtitle: Two French-trimmed racks fashioned into a crown, roasted pink
excerpt: A crown of lamb is two French-trimmed racks bent into a ring with the bones pointing upward, filled with a herb or apricot stuffing, and roasted at high heat to a blush-pink centre. It is a showpiece that takes less skill than its appearance suggests.
sourceNotes: Traditional British celebratory roasting. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): crown of lamb assembly and stuffing options. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): French-trimmed rack preparation. Cross-referenced across multiple sources.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The crown can be assembled, stuffed, and refrigerated 24 hours ahead. Bring to room temperature 1 hour before roasting.
recipe.temperatureNote: high throughout for a short roast

glossaryTerms.definition: A rack of lamb with the meat and fat scraped away from the exposed rib bones above the eye, producing clean white bones that present elegantly at the table. Also called Frenching.
Two or more French-trimmed racks of lamb or pork bent into a circle with the bones pointing outward and upward, tied with twine to hold the shape, and roasted as a single showpiece joint.

content.text: is assembled from two racks that have been

by the butcher. The difficulty lies in the assembly, not the cooking; the roasting itself is a short affair at 220°C to an internal temperature of 58°C for pink, 62°C for medium. Ask your butcher to Frenchthe racks; assembling the crown yourself from pre-prepared racks takes about 10 minutes.

The stuffing fills the cavity in the centre of the crown, bakes inside the ring, and slices out with each cutlet. A simple mixture of breadcrumbs, dried apricot, and herbs works well; it contrasts with the richness of the lamb without competing with it.

items.prepNote: French-trimmed, 6-7 cutlets each, prepared by butcher

content.text: Stand both racks bone-side outward in a circle, flesh facing inward. Use kitchen twine to tie the crown securely at two points around the circumference, pulling the ends of the racks together until the circle holds. Wrap foil around the exposed bone tips to prevent scorching. Brush all surfaces with

with the chopped apricots, parsley, rosemary leaves, and melted butter. Season and pack loosely into the crown cavity. Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Roast for 25-35 minutes until the eye of the rack reads 58°C for pink, 62°C for medium-pink.

Remove the foil from the bones, transfer the crown to a warm board, and rest for 15 minutes. Cut away the twine. Spoon the stuffing out first, then cut down between the ribs to produce individual cutlets. Deglaze the roasting pan with
and strain the gravy into a warm jug.

rows.symptom: The crown falls apart when the racks meet
rows.cause: Racks not tied tightly enough, or the bones are too short to hold the arc
rows.fix: Tie at three points rather than two, and ask the butcher to score the chine bone slightly to help the racks bend.

rows.symptom: Bone tips are scorched despite foil
rows.cause: Foil slipped or was too thin
rows.fix: Fold a double layer of foil and press firmly onto the tips. Remove for the last 5 minutes only if you want colour on the bones.
Pack more firmly. A small ramekin placed in the cavity before filling can hold the shape during roasting, then removed when the stuffing sets.

rows.symptom: Outer cutlets overcooked while inner ones are rare
rows.cause: Probe was at the outer wall rather than the inner eye
rows.fix: Probe from the top down into the thick part of each rack's eye, not the thinner outer edge.

content.text: The crown of lamb became a feature of the British celebratory table in the mid-twentieth century, appearing in Constance Spry's menus and later in Delia Smith's party repertoire. It suits Easter and spring entertaining, and the visual impact justifies the modest extra effort in assembly. Each guest receives two or three cutlets with a spoonful of stuffing and the pan gravy.
